content:
{
  "title": "Semantic Error-Aware Prompting for Robust Code Generation with LLMs",
  "problem": "Large language models often generate code that passes syntactic checks but fails to satisfy deeper semantic constraints, leading to functional bugs and unreliable code completions. Existing prompting methods do not explicitly guide models to reason about or validate semantic correctness during generation.",
  "motivation": "As LLMs are increasingly deployed for real-world code generation, ensuring semantic correctness is critical for safe and effective automation. Prevailing approaches mainly address syntax or high-level reasoning, neglecting the gap between code that 'looks correct' and code that 'works as intended.' Addressing this gap can unlock LLMs' ability to produce higher-quality, production-ready code.",
  "method": "We propose Semantic Error-Aware Prompting (SEAP), a two-stage prompting framework for LLMs. In stage 1, the prompt asks the model to enumerate and briefly describe potential semantic errors relevant to the programming task (e.g., off-by-one, data type misuse, logic errors). In stage 2, the model is prompted to generate code while explicitly referencing and mitigating the earlier enumerated semantic risks, using natural language annotations inline with code. The framework is model-agnostic and encourages explicit semantic self-monitoring during code synthesis.",
  "experiment_plan": "We will evaluate SEAP on HumanEval, MBPP, and APPS datasets, comparing against baseline prompting (standard and Chain-of-Thought) and recent planning/self-revision methods. Metrics include functional correctness (pass@k), semantic bug rate (via static analysis and human review), and code quality ratings. Ablation studies will analyze the impact of each stage of SEAP."
}
